title: Dog Frequent Urination in Small Amounts: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog urinating frequently in small amounts? Learn about potential causes like UTIs, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Frequent urination in small amounts usually indicates bladder irritation and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours; however, a complete inability to urinate is a life-threatening emergency.

Why is my dog trying to urinate frequently but only producing a few drops?

When a dog attempts to urinate frequently but only produces a few drops, it is often a sign of lower urinary tract irritation. This behavior, known as pollakiuria, suggests that the bladder lining is inflamed, making the dog feel like they need to go even when the bladder is nearly empty. Common causes include urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, or crystals.

How urgent is it if my dog is frequently urinating small amounts?

  • This condition is generally classified as a medium urgency level.
  • While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is very uncomfortable and often painful for your pet.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours.
  • If your dog is straining and producing absolutely no urine, this indicates a life-threatening blockage and requires immediate emergency care.

How does taking a photo of my dog's urine help with veterinary triage?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a photo of the urine—ideally on a light-colored surface or a puppy pad—can be incredibly helpful for triage.
  • Visual evidence of blood (hematuria), cloudiness, or unusual sediment allows the veterinary team to assess the severity of the inflammation.
  • This information helps the clinic prioritize your dog's care more effectively.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Frequent attempts to urinate with stranguria and passage of only small amounts of urine can indicate failure of normal voiding. This can be due to mechanical obstruction of the urethra by calculi, neoplasms, or strictures; detrusor atony from overdistention of the bladder; or neurologic disease. Paradoxical urinary incontinence may develop when there is a partial obstruction of the urethra leading to bladder distention and overflow incontinence. Neurologic causes of micturition disorders can be categorized as upper or lower motor neuron lesions. Lesions in the sacral spinal cord, pelvic nerve, and detrusor atony lead to lower motor neuron signs, which are often characterized by a distended, easily expressed bladder. Cystitis and crystalluria should also be eliminated as differential diagnoses.

Chapter: Nephrology, Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1514)

faqs.question: Is frequent urination a sign of a UTI?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Urinary Tract Infection is one of the most common reasons a dog will strain to produce small amounts of urine frequently.

faqs.question: What if there is blood in the urine?
faqs.answer: Blood in the urine indicates significant inflammation or trauma to the bladder wall and should be evaluated by a vet as soon as possible.

faqs.question: Can bladder stones cause these symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes, bladder stones can irritate the bladder wall or partially obstruct the urethra, leading to frequent, small-volume urination.
